description.crust: The outermost layer of Earth. Continental crust is 30–70 km thick; oceanic crust is 5–10 km. P-wave: 6.0 km/s, S-wave: 3.5 km/s.
description.upperMantle: Extends from the crust to ~400 km depth. Includes the lithosphere and asthenosphere. P-wave: 8.0 km/s, S-wave: 4.5 km/s.
description.lowerMantle: From 400 to 2900 km depth. Solid rock under high pressure. P-wave: 13.0 km/s, S-wave: 7.0 km/s.
description.outerCore: From 2900 to 5150 km depth. Liquid iron-nickel alloy. S-waves cannot travel through liquids, creating the S-wave shadow zone. P-wave: 9.0 km/s.
description.innerCore: From 5150 km to Earth's center. Solid iron-nickel alloy. P-wave: 11.0 km/s, S-wave: 3.5 km/s.

explanation.intro: Click on the Earth to set an epicenter and watch seismic waves propagate through Earth's interior. Blue = P-waves, Red = S-waves.
explanation.shadowZoneS: No S-waves reach stations beyond 103°. S-waves (shear waves) cannot travel through liquid, so they are absorbed by the liquid outer core. The S-wave shadow zone is evidence that Earth's outer core is liquid.
explanation.shadowZoneP: Between 103° and 143° lies the P-wave shadow zone. The large velocity change at the core-mantle boundary bends P-waves away from this region. PKP waves (P-waves through the core) emerge beyond 143°.
explanation.pkpArrival: PKP wave detected! This P-wave traveled through the outer core and emerged on the far side of Earth, beyond the shadow zone.
explanation.observerInShadow: This station ({{distance}}°) is in the S-wave shadow zone. Only P-waves (or PKP) can reach it.
explanation.bothWaves: This station ({{distance}}°) receives both P-waves and S-waves. P-waves arrive first, followed by S-waves.
explanation.pFirst: P-waves (Primary waves) travel faster than S-waves and arrive first at every distance. S-waves (Secondary waves) travel only through solid material.

q1.text: Which seismic waves reach a station at an epicentral distance of 120°?
q1.explanation: Beyond 103°, S-waves cannot arrive (absorbed by the liquid outer core). The region 103°–143° is also the P direct-wave shadow zone, but PKP waves (core-crossing P-waves) can arrive beyond ~143°. In simplified educational models, PKP arrival is considered for this zone.

q2.text: Why does the S-wave shadow zone exist?
q2.explanation: S-waves are shear (transverse) waves that cannot travel through liquids. The outer core is composed of liquid iron-nickel alloy, so S-waves are absorbed there. This creates the S-wave shadow zone beyond ~103°.

q3.text: At an epicentral distance of 80°, which wave arrives first?
q3.explanation: P-waves always travel faster than S-waves (e.g., P: 6.0 km/s vs S: 3.5 km/s in the crust). P-waves always arrive first — hence the names Primary (P) and Secondary (S) waves.

q4.text: What is the main reason seismic waves curve (refract) as they travel through Earth?
q4.explanation: By Snell's Law, waves refract at boundaries where velocity changes. Inside Earth, increasing pressure and temperature change wave velocities continuously with depth, causing seismic rays to curve rather than travel in straight lines.
